form.errorNoCode: Enter the batch code from your product.

brandPage.checkerTitle: Verificador de código de lote de {name}
brandPage.intro: Introduce el código de lote de {name} abajo para descifrar al instante la fecha de fabricación, la antigüedad y la caducidad estimada: gratis y privado.
brandPage.howHeading: Cómo funcionan los códigos de lote de {name}
brandPage.freshnessPara: Once we read the manufacture date, we estimate freshness using {name}'s typical {shelf}-month shelf life for {category} products. Remember that the period-after-opening (about {pao} months) becomes the limit once you start using the product.
brandPage.workedExample: A {name} batch code of {code} decodes to a manufacture date of {date}, with an estimated expiration around {shelf} months later.
brandPage.aliasesPara: You'll sometimes see this tool called a {name} batch code decoder, {name} batch code checker, or {name} batch code calculator — they all do the same thing: read the production code and tell you how fresh your {name} {category} is.

decoderExplain.estee-lauder: Estée Lauder group codes are 3 characters: the first is the manufacturing plant, the second is the month (1–9 = January–September, A/B/C = October–December), and the third is the last digit of the year.
decoderExplain.loreal: L'Oréal group codes are usually 6 characters (e.g. 22U401). The leading digits are the factory, the first letter is the production year (an annual letter cycle where 'V' is skipped, so U = 2021, W = 2022, X = 2023…), and the character right after it is the month (1–9 = January–September, O/N/D = October/November/December). The last three characters are the production batch and have no bearing on shelf life.
decoderExplain.coty: Coty fragrances carry a 4-digit code (e.g. 4135) on the box base or the clear sticker under the bottle. The first digit is the last digit of the production year, and the remaining three are the day of that year — so 4135 is the 135th day of 2024, i.e. 14 May 2024.
decoderExplain.chanel: Chanel does not publish a fixed batch-code cipher, but current Chanel fragrances, makeup and skincare print the production date in the code — typically the last digit of the year followed by the day of the year (e.g. 4135 = day 135 of 2024). Some older or region-specific codes cannot be decoded automatically.
decoderExplain.dior: Dior and its sister LVMH houses print the production date in the batch code — usually the last digit of the year followed by the day of the year (e.g. 4135 = the 135th day of 2024), sometimes as a 5- or 6-digit date. There is no separate month/plant cipher to memorise.
decoderExplain.julian: A large share of manufacturers print the date directly in the batch code — either as a Julian date (year + day-of-year, e.g. 24045 = the 45th day of 2024) or as a packed calendar date (YYMMDD / DDMMYY).
decoderExplain.auto: {name} is manufactured under {group}. Its batch codes usually embed the production date directly — either as a Julian date (year plus day-of-year) or a packed calendar date. Our checker auto-detects the format and estimates the manufacture date.

storageTip.skincare: Keep it out of direct sunlight and away from heat, and close caps tightly. Bathrooms get humid, so a cool, dry drawer is better — especially for active ingredients like vitamin C, retinol or acids, which oxidise faster once exposed to air and light.
storageTip.makeup: Store it away from heat and moisture and keep liquids and mascara tightly closed. Sharpen pencils, wash brushes and avoid adding water or saliva, which introduce bacteria and shorten how long the product stays safe to use.
storageTip.perfume: Keep the bottle in its box, away from light, heat and humidity. Avoid storing it in the bathroom — heat and steam break down the fragrance oils faster than age alone, dulling the scent and shifting the colour.
storageTip.haircare: Keep bottles closed and out of direct shower heat and steam when not in use, and store them somewhere cool and dry. Water left inside pumps or caps can encourage mould over time.
storageTip.suncare: Store it below 25°C, out of direct sun, and never leave it in a hot car or on the beach for hours — heat degrades UV filters, so an expired or overheated sunscreen protects less than a fresh one.
storageTip.generic: Store it somewhere cool and dry, out of direct sunlight, and keep the container tightly closed between uses.

brandFaq.q_read: How do I read a {name} batch code?
brandFaq.q_example: What does a {name} batch code like {code} mean?
brandFaq.a_example: A {name} batch code of {code} decodes to a manufacture date of {date}. From there we add {name}'s typical {shelf}-month shelf life to estimate when the unopened product is best used by.
brandFaq.q_where: Where is the batch code on {name} products?
brandFaq.a_where: Look for a short stamped, embossed or ink-jet code — usually on the base of the bottle or jar, the crimp at the end of a tube, or the bottom or flap of the box. On {name} {noun} it is separate from the barcode and typically 3–8 characters of letters and numbers.
brandFaq.q_barcode: What's the difference between the {name} batch code and the barcode?
brandFaq.a_barcode: The barcode is the long striped EAN/UPC number used at checkout and is the same for every unit of a product. The {name} batch code is a short production code that changes with each manufacturing run and encodes when the item was made — that's the one this tool decodes.
brandFaq.q_expired: How do I know if my {name} product is expired?
brandFaq.a_expired: Enter your {name} batch code above to get the manufacture date, then compare it to the typical {shelf}-month unopened shelf life. If more time has passed than that — or more than about {pao} months since you opened it — treat the product as expired, especially if the texture, colour or smell has changed.
brandFaq.q_unopened: How long do unopened {name} products last?
brandFaq.a_unopened: Unopened {name} {noun} typically stays good for about {shelf} months from the manufacture date when stored properly. This is an industry estimate — {name} does not print a hard expiry date on most items, which is why the batch code matters.
brandFaq.q_pao: How long does {name} last after opening (PAO)?
brandFaq.a_pao: Once opened, {name} {noun} should generally be used within about {pao} months — this is the Period After Opening, shown as the open-jar symbol (e.g. {pao}M) on the packaging. After you open a product, the PAO becomes the limit even if the batch itself is still fresh.
brandFaq.q_paoSymbol: What does the open-jar (PAO) symbol on {name} mean?
brandFaq.a_paoSymbol: The little open-jar icon with a number and an "M" (for months) is the Period After Opening. For most {name} {noun} it's around {pao}M, meaning roughly {pao} months of safe use from the day you first open it. The batch code tells you how fresh it was before opening; the PAO tells you how long it lasts after.
brandFaq.q_afterExpiry: Can I still use {name} after the expiration date?
brandFaq.a_afterExpiry: Using {name} {noun} shortly past its estimated date is usually a cosmetic-quality issue rather than a safety one — actives lose potency, textures separate and fragrances fade. Stop using it if you notice a change in smell, colour or consistency, and never use eye or lip products that are old, as bacteria risk is higher.
brandFaq.q_store: How should I store {name} {noun} to keep it fresh?
brandFaq.q_fake: Can a batch code tell me if my {name} is fake?
brandFaq.a_fake: A batch code decoder reads the production date — on its own it can't prove a product is genuine. But a {name} code that won't decode, or a date that doesn't match when or where you bought it, can be a red flag. For authenticity, also check the packaging, seals and font, and buy from authorised {group} retailers.
brandFaq.q_accuracy: How accurate is this {name} batch code decoder?
brandFaq.a_accuracy: We read the manufacture date using {decoder}. Where {group} publishes a known code scheme the result is precise to the month or day; where the brand doesn't confirm a public scheme it's a best-estimate reading. The date is the manufacture date, not a guaranteed expiry — shelf-life figures are typical industry averages.
brandFaq.q_free: Is the {name} batch code checker free?
brandFaq.a_free: Yes — decoding {name} batch codes on Cosmetics Batch is completely free, works instantly in your browser with no sign-up, and your codes are never stored or sent to a server.

homeFaq.a1: A batch code is a short code printed on cosmetic and perfume packaging that identifies the production run. Decoding it reveals when the product was manufactured.
homeFaq.q2: Is this batch code checker free?
homeFaq.a2: Yes. Cosmetics Batch is completely free, requires no account, and decodes codes directly in your browser.
homeFaq.a3: We use manufacturer-specific algorithms where available and honest confidence levels elsewhere. Shelf-life estimates are typical values, not guarantees of safety.
homeFaq.q4: Where do I find my batch code?
homeFaq.a4: It's usually a short stamped or embossed code on the base of a bottle, the crimp of a tube, or the bottom of the box — separate from the barcode.
homeFaq.q5: Does an expired product mean it's unsafe?
homeFaq.a5: Not necessarily. It means the product is past its typical shelf life and may have reduced performance. Eye products and anything that smells or looks off should be discarded.
homeFaq.q6: What does the open-jar (PAO) symbol mean?
homeFaq.a6: The little open-jar icon with a number like 12M or 24M is the Period After Opening — how many months a product stays good once you first open it. After opening, this becomes the limit even if the batch code date is recent.
homeFaq.q7: What's the difference between shelf life and expiration date?
homeFaq.a7: Most cosmetics don't print an expiry date. The batch code gives the manufacture date, and we estimate the expiration by adding the product's typical unopened shelf life — usually 24–36 months for makeup and skincare, and 3–5 years for perfume.
homeFaq.a8: Most fragrances last 3 to 5 years from the manufacture date, and often longer if stored cool and away from light. Perfume rarely becomes unsafe, but oxidation slowly changes the scent — the top notes fade first.
homeFaq.q9: Why can't my batch code be decoded?
homeFaq.a9: Some brands change their coding over time, use region-specific formats, or don't encode the date publicly. Double-check you entered the stamped batch code (not the barcode), or try selecting the parent company instead of the sub-brand.
homeFaq.q10: Do you store my batch codes or personal data?
homeFaq.a10: No. Codes are decoded in your browser and we don't require an account. Nothing you type is saved to a server, so checking a code is completely private.
